nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and semi-sheltered seaward reefs; juveniles often found among Acropora corals. Depth range typically 1–50 m, with records to 280 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented eating algae, small invertebrates, mollusks, sponges, polychaetes, and crustaceans in the wild. In captivity, newly introduced individuals tend to accept live brine shrimp or bloodworms initially; once acclimated, the diet can typically be transitioned to chopped squid, clams, mussels, and frozen herbivore preparations.

goodWith: fish too large to be threatened

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 1914x1436. Front three-quarter view of a vivid yellow juvenile showing the iconic cube shape and bold black polka-dot pattern; tack-sharp, vibrant, whole animal in frame - the classic in-trade Yellow Boxfish look.
Rickard Zerpe; 3622x2717. Sharp full lateral profile of a sub-adult in reef habitat, yellow-brown body with black-and-white spotting; shows the transitional coloration and boxy body form.

J. Petersen; 2210x1581. Sharp full lateral view of a mature adult with blue-grey body, yellow honeycomb reticulation and yellow snout/tail base; represents the adult colour phase described in the care notes.

summary: The Cubicus Boxfish (Ostracion cubicum) is a striking Indo-Pacific reef species known for its vivid yellow juvenile coloration with brown spots and its ability to release ostracitoxin — a potent skin toxin — when stressed or dying. It is widely considered suitable only for experienced to expert-level aquarists.
careNotes:
The Cubicus Boxfish is widely considered suitable only for very experienced aquarists, and its defining care challenge is a chemical defence mechanism that few other aquarium fish share. When stressed or dying, it releases ostracitoxin from specialized mucous glands in its skin — a toxin that has been reported to kill every other fish in a closed system within a very short period. Minimizing stress at every stage, from handling and transport through acclimation and day-to-day husbandry, is therefore the central concern of keeping this species.

Juveniles display vivid yellow bodies covered in brown spots, which is the form most commonly encountered in the trade. As adults, the coloration tends to shift: the body fades to a more muted brown, with yellow spotting taking over as the dominant pattern element. In the wild, the Cubicus Boxfish inhabits lagoon and semi-sheltered seaward reefs across a broad Indo-Pacific range, including the Red Sea, East Africa, Hawaii, and as far south as Lord Howe Island.

Hobbyist care guides and wild-collected data differ somewhat on ideal temperature, with recommendations ranging from roughly 22 to 29°C across sources; a midpoint within that range is generally considered appropriate for captive care.

The Cubicus Boxfish is solitary and benthopelagic (living and feeding near the seafloor rather than in open water) in the wild. It is generally not considered safe in reef tanks, as it tends to nip at tubeworms and will prey on small invertebrates, shrimps, and crabs. Suitable tankmates are typically large, semi-aggressive fish that are not small enough to be threatened by the boxfish and are themselves robust enough to handle an environment where toxin release, however rare under good conditions, remains a possibility.

In the wild, reproduction is reported to occur in harems, with a single male presiding over two to four females. The species is not protogynous; males maintain harems over females rather than females changing sex to assume the male role. Breeding in captivity has rarely been achieved.
breedingNotes: Very little success has been reported in breeding the Cubicus Boxfish in captivity. Wild populations are reported to reproduce in harems, with a single male presiding over two to four females.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (45 cm max) and temperature range (24.5–29°C wild data), taxonomy, native range, and habitat. FishBase temperature range is warmer than hobby-source recommendations; averaged across sources.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Additional cross-check on size (18 in / ~45.7 cm), temperature (72–78°F), salinity (1.020–1.025), tank size (125 gal / 473 L), and diet. Consistent with LiveAquaria standard page on most parameters.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Additional cross-check; cites max size 45 cm, minimum tank 800 L, salinity ~1.026. Tank size recommendation is notably higher than hobby sources; factored into averaged minimum volume.
